long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple sequential steps, reagents, or transformations.

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions requiring counting of molecules, ions, bonds, or specific numerical calculations.

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms, intermediates, or transition states.

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties (acidity, basicity, reactivity, etc.) and arranging in orders.

questions.question_text: The role of a catalyst is to change: (A) equilibrium constant (B) enthalpy of reaction (C) Gibbs energy of reaction (D) activation energy of reaction
questions.solution_summary: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of a reaction by providing an alternative pathway. It does not change equilibrium constant, enthalpy, or Gibbs energy.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of the definition and role of a catalyst.

questions.question_text: Which of the following molecules is chiral in nature? (A) 1-chloropropane (B) 2-chloropropane (C) 1-chlorobutane (D) 2-chlorobutane
questions.solution_summary: 2-chlorobutane has a chiral carbon (C2 attached to -CH3, -C2H5, -Cl, and -H). The other compounds do not have a carbon with four different groups.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identifying the presence of a chiral carbon (four different groups).

questions.question_text: CH3CH2OH can be converted to CH3CHO by: (A) catalytic hydrogenation (B) treatment with LiAlH4 (C) treatment with PCC (D) treatment with KMnO4
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.solution_summary: Primary alcohols are oxidised to aldehydes using mild oxidising agents like PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate). KMnO4 would further oxidise to carboxylic acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of specific oxidising agents for selective oxidation of primary alcohols to aldehydes.

questions.question_text: The IUPAC name for CH3-CH2-N(CH3)-CH2-CH2-CH3 is: (A) N-methylpentan-2-amine (B) N-ethyl-N-methylpropan-1-amine (C) N,N-diethylpropan-1-amine (D) N,N-dimethylpropan-1-amine
questions.solution_summary: The longest chain containing N is propan-1-amine. The substituents on N are ethyl and methyl. Alphabetically, ethyl comes before methyl, so N-ethyl-N-methylpropan-1-amine.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires systematic application of IUPAC nomenclature rules for amines.

questions.question_text: A plot between concentration of reactant [R] and time 't' is shown below. Which of the given order of reaction is indicated by the graph? [Graph: Linear decreasing plot] (A) Third order (B) Second order (C) First order (D) Zero order
questions.solution_summary: For a zero order reaction, [R] vs t gives a straight line with negative slope. Rate = k, independent of concentration.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recalling the graphical relationship between concentration and time for different reaction orders.

questions.question_text: The treatment of ethyl bromide with alcoholic silver nitrite gives: (A) ethyl nitrite (B) nitroethane (C) nitromethane (D) ethene
questions.solution_summary: Ethyl bromide reacts with alcoholic silver nitrite to give nitroethane (C2H5NO2) via nucleophilic substitution. The nitro group (NO2) attaches to the carbon.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of the reaction of alkyl halides with silver nitrite.

questions.question_text: Which of the following aqueous solutions will have the highest freezing point? (A) 1.0 M KCl (B) 1.0 M Na2SO4 (C) 1.0 M Glucose (D) 1.0 M AlCl3
questions.solution_summary: Freezing point depression is a colligative property: ΔTf = i × Kf × m. Glucose does not dissociate (i=1), so it has the smallest depression and thus the highest freezing point.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparing van't Hoff factors and understanding their effect on colligative properties.

questions.question_text: Which of the following aldehydes will undergo Cannizzaro reaction? [Image: benzaldehyde, acetaldehyde, propanal, butanal]
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.solution_summary: Cannizzaro reaction is undergone by aldehydes that do not have an alpha-hydrogen atom. Benzaldehyde (C6H5CHO) lacks alpha-H, so it undergoes Cannizzaro.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identifying the structural requirement (no alpha-H) for Cannizzaro reaction.

questions.question_text: In which of the following groups are both ions coloured in aqueous solution? I. Cu+ II. Ti4+ III. Co2+ IV. Fe2+ [Atomic number: Cu=29, Ti=22, Co=27, Fe=26] (A) I and II (B) II and III (C) III and IV (D) I and IV
questions.solution_summary: Coloured ions have partially filled d-orbitals. Cu+ (d10) is colourless. Ti4+ (d0) is colourless. Co2+ (d7) and Fe2+ (d6) are coloured.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recalling d-electron configurations and determining which ions have unpaired electrons causing colour.

questions.question_text: CH3CH2CHO and CH3CH2COOH can be distinguished by: (A) Sodium bicarbonate test (B) Hinsberg test (C) Iodoform test (D) Lucas test
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.solution_summary: Carboxylic acids react with NaHCO3 to liberate CO2 (effervescence). Aldehydes do not. Hinsberg is for amines, iodoform is for methyl ketones, Lucas is for alcohols.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of functional group tests to distinguish aldehydes from carboxylic acids.

questions.question_text: Match the type of cell given in Column I with their use given in Column II. i. Lead storage cell → a. Wall clock ii. Mercury cell → b. Apollo Space Programme iii. Dry cell → c. Wrist watch iv. Fuel cell → d. Inverter
questions.sub_topic: Types of Cells and Their Uses
questions.solution_summary: Lead storage cells are used in inverters. Mercury cells are used in wrist watches. Dry cells are used in wall clocks. Fuel cells are used in Apollo Space Programme.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires matching electrochemical cells with their applications.

questions.question_text: Abhishek added yellow coloured potassium chromate solution into a test tube. The colour changed to orange. Which of the following substances will be the most likely liquid to be present in the test tube? (A) Sodium hydrogen carbonate solution (B) Methyl orange solution (C) Sodium hydroxide solution (D) HCl solution
questions.solution_summary: Yellow chromate (CrO4^2-) converts to orange dichromate (Cr2O7^2-) in acidic medium. HCl provides acidic medium.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the chromate-dichromate equilibrium and the effect of acid.

questions.question_text: Assertion: For measuring resistance of an ionic solution an AC source is used. Reason: Concentration of ionic solution will change if DC source is used.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: AC is used to avoid electrolysis. Reason is true and explains it: DC would cause electrolysis, changing the concentration and composition of the solution.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of why AC is used in conductivity measurements to prevent electrolysis.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Henry's law constant (KH) decreases with increase in temperature. Reason: As the temperature increases, solubility of gases in liquids decreases.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is false: KH increases with increase in temperature. Reason is true: solubility of gases decreases with increase in temperature.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the temperature dependence of Henry's constant and gas solubility.

questions.question_text: Assertion: The solubility of aldehydes and ketones in water decreases with increase in size of the alkyl group. Reason: Aldehydes and ketones have dipole-dipole interaction.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: larger alkyl groups are hydrophobic, reducing water solubility. Reason is also true: aldehydes and ketones have dipole-dipole interactions, but this does not explain the trend with size. The correct explanation is the increasing hydrophobic character.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the factors affecting solubility (hydrophobic effect, dipole interactions) and their relative importance.

questions.question_text: Assertion: The boiling points of alkyl halides decrease in the order RI > RBr > RCl > RF. Reason: The van der Waals forces of attraction decrease in the order RI > RBr > RCl > RF.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: boiling points increase with size of halogen. Reason is true and explains it: larger halogen atoms have more electrons, leading to stronger van der Waals forces.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the trend in boiling points of alkyl halides and its explanation via van der Waals forces.

questions.question_text: (a) Calculate the elevation of boiling point of a solution when 3g of CaCl2 (Molar mass = 111 g mol-1) was dissolved in 260g of water, assuming that CaCl2 undergoes complete dissociation. (Kb for water = 0.52 K kg mol-1)
questions.sub_topic: Colligative Properties - Elevation of Boiling Point
questions.solution_summary: CaCl2 dissociates into 3 ions (Ca2+ + 2Cl-), so van't Hoff factor i = 3. Molality = (3/111)/(260/1000) = 0.1038 mol/kg. ΔTb = i × Kb × m.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of boiling point elevation formula with van't Hoff factor.

questions.question_text: Based on the data given above, give plausible reason for the variation of conductivity and molar conductivity with concentration. [Table: KCl conductivity decreases with dilution, molar conductivity increases with dilution]
questions.correct_answer: Conductivity decreases with dilution because the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Molar conductivity increases with dilution because the volume containing one mole of electrolyte increases, and interionic attractions decrease.
questions.solution_summary: Explanation of the concentration dependence of conductivity and molar conductivity.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the underlying reasons for the variation of conductivity and molar conductivity with concentration.

questions.question_text: (a) Write the rate law expression for the reaction 2HI → H2 + I2, if the order of the reaction is zero. (b) What is the effect of temperature on the rate of reaction? Write a mathematical expression for the same.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Rate = k (since zero order, rate is independent of concentration). (b) Rate increases with temperature. Arrhenius equation: k = Ae^(-Ea/RT) or ln k = ln A - Ea/RT.
questions.solution_summary: Zero order rate law and Arrhenius equation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of rate law for zero order and the Arrhenius equation.

questions.question_text: Explain the mechanism of acid catalysed hydration of ethene.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.correct_answer: Step 1: Protonation of ethene by H+ forms a carbocation (more stable secondary or tertiary if applicable). Step 2: Water attacks the carbocation to form protonated alcohol. Step 3: Deprotonation gives ethanol. Mechanism proceeds via Markovnikov rule.
questions.solution_summary: Mechanism of acid-catalysed hydration: protonation, nucleophilic attack, deprotonation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires detailed stepwise mechanistic understanding of acid-catalysed hydration.

questions.question_text: (a) How can acetaldehyde be prepared from acetyl chloride? (b) Propanal is more reactive than propanone towards nucleophilic addition reaction. Give reason.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.sub_topic: Preparation and Reactivity of Carbonyl Compounds
questions.correct_answer: (a) Acetyl chloride is reduced with H2 in presence of Pd-BaSO4 (Rosenmund reduction) to give acetaldehyde. (b) Propanal is more reactive than propanone due to less steric hindrance (only one alkyl group) and electronic factors (aldehydes have less electron donation to the carbonyl carbon).
questions.solution_summary: Rosenmund reduction for preparation of aldehydes, and comparison of aldehyde vs ketone reactivity.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of Rosenmund reduction and comparison of aldehyde vs ketone reactivity.

questions.question_text: (a) Shweta mixed two liquids A and B of 10 mL each. After mixing, the volume of the solution was found to be 20.2 mL. (i) Why was there a volume change after mixing the liquids? (ii) Will there be an increase or decrease of temperature after mixing? (iii) Give one example for this type of solution.
questions.correct_answer: (i) Volume increases (20.2 mL > 20 mL) due to positive deviation from Raoult's law (weaker A-B interactions than A-A and B-B). (ii) Temperature decreases (endothermic mixing, ΔH > 0). (iii) Example: ethanol + acetone (or carbon disulphide + acetone).
questions.solution_summary: Positive deviation leads to volume expansion and endothermic mixing.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the molecular basis of positive deviation from Raoult's law.

questions.question_text: For the reaction A + B → Products, the following initial rates were obtained at various initial concentrations of reactants: [Table: [A]=0.1, [B]=0.1 → Rate=0.05; [A]=0.2, [B]=0.1 → Rate=0.10; [A]=0.1, [B]=0.2 → Rate=0.05]. Determine the order of the reaction with respect to A and B and overall order of the reaction.
questions.sub_topic: Determination of Order from Initial Rate Data
questions.correct_answer: When [A] doubles (0.1 to 0.2) keeping [B] constant, rate doubles (0.05 to 0.10) → order w.r.t A = 1. When [B] doubles (0.1 to 0.2) keeping [A] constant, rate remains same (0.05) → order w.r.t B = 0. Overall order = 1 + 0 = 1.
questions.solution_summary: Rate law: Rate = k[A]^1[B]^0 = k[A]. Overall order = 1.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires analysis of initial rate data to determine orders and rate law.

questions.question_text: (a) What is meant by crystal field splitting energy? For a d4 ion, write the configuration if (i) Δ0 < P and (ii) Δ0 > P. (b) Explain why in tetrahedral coordination entities, low spin configurations are rarely observed.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Crystal field splitting energy (Δ0) is the energy difference between t2g and eg orbitals in octahedral field. d4: (i) Δ0 < P → high spin: t2g^3 eg^1 (4 unpaired). (ii) Δ0 > P → low spin: t2g^4 eg^0 (2 unpaired). (b) Tetrahedral splitting (Δt) is much smaller than octahedral (Δt ≈ 4/9 Δ0), so pairing energy is rarely exceeded, leading to high spin configurations.
questions.solution_summary: CFT splitting and electronic configurations for d4 in octahedral field, and reason for rarity of low spin tetrahedral complexes.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of CFT, electron configurations, and comparison of octahedral vs tetrahedral splitting.

questions.question_text: Account for the following: (a) The C-Cl bond length in chlorobenzene is shorter than that in methyl chloride. (b) Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions. (c) In case of optically active alkyl halides, SN1 reactions are accompanied by racemisation.
questions.correct_answer: (a) In chlorobenzene, the C-Cl bond has partial double bond character due to resonance, making it shorter. (b) Grignard reagents react with water (and other protic solvents) to give alkanes, so they must be prepared under anhydrous conditions. (c) SN1 proceeds via a planar carbocation intermediate, which can be attacked from either side, giving a racemic mixture.
questions.solution_summary: Reasons for bond length, need for anhydrous conditions, and racemisation in SN1.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires mechanistic understanding of resonance in aryl halides, Grignard reagent sensitivity, and SN1 racemisation.

questions.question_text: (a) Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their acidic strength: 3,5-dinitrophenol, 4-methylphenol, phenol, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol. (b) What happens when: (write equations) (i) Phenol is distilled with Zn dust? (ii) Anisole is treated with HBr?
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.sub_topic: Acidity and Reactions of Phenols & Ethers
questions.correct_answer: (a) 4-methylphenol < phenol < 3,5-dinitrophenol < 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (increasing acidity). (b)(i) Phenol + Zn → Benzene + ZnO. (ii) Anisole + HBr → Phenol + CH3Br (cleavage of ether).
questions.solution_summary: Effect of substituents on acidity of phenols, and reactions of phenol and anisole.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires ordering phenols based on substituent effects and recalling reactions of phenol and anisole.

questions.question_text: An organic compound 'A' (molecular formula C8H8O) gives 2,4-DNP test. It does not give Tollen's test, but gives a yellow precipitate 'B' with NaOH and I2. On drastic oxidation, it gives a carboxylic acid 'C' with formula C7H6O2. Identify 'A', 'B', 'C' and write the reactions involved.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.correct_answer: A = Acetophenone (C6H5COCH3). B = Iodoform (CHI3). C = Benzoic acid (C6H5COOH). Reactions: A gives 2,4-DNP (carbonyl). No Tollens (not aldehyde). Iodoform test (methyl ketone). Oxidation of acetophenone gives benzoic acid.
questions.solution_summary: Stepwise identification: 2,4-DNP confirms carbonyl; no Tollens → not aldehyde; iodoform → methyl ketone; oxidation → benzoic acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires connecting multiple qualitative tests and reactions to identify an unknown compound.

questions.question_text: (a) Name the type of linkage responsible for the formation of proteins from α-amino acids. (b) Write any two differences between DNA and RNA.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Peptide linkage (-CO-NH-). (b) Differences: DNA has deoxyribose sugar, RNA has ribose sugar; DNA has thymine base, RNA has uracil; DNA is double-stranded, RNA is single-stranded.
questions.solution_summary: Peptide bond definition and differences between DNA and RNA.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of peptide linkage and comparison of DNA vs RNA.

questions.question_text: Case: Carbohydrates. (a) Give chemical reactions to show the presence of an aldehyde group and straight chain in glucose. (b) (i) Define anomers. OR (ii) Draw the structure of β-D-Glucopyranose. (c) Sucrose is known as invert sugar. Explain.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Aldehyde group: glucose gives Tollens' test (silver mirror) and Fehling's test (red precipitate). Straight chain: glucose with HI gives n-hexane. (b)(i) Anomers are cyclic diastereomers that differ only at the anomeric carbon (C1). OR structure of β-D-glucopyranose. (c) Sucrose on hydrolysis gives equimolar glucose and fructose, and the optical rotation changes from dextro (+) to laevo (-), hence called invert sugar.
questions.solution_summary: Chemical tests for functional groups in glucose, definition of anomers, and explanation of invert sugar.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of glucose structure elucidation, anomers, and sucrose hydrolysis.

questions.question_text: Case: Werner's coordination theory. (a) One mole of CrCl3·4H2O precipitates one mole of AgCl when treated with excess of AgNO3 solution. Write (i) the structural formula of the complex, and (ii) the secondary valency of Cr. (b) What is the difference between a complex and a double salt? (c) (i) Arrange the following complexes in the increasing order of conductivity of their solution: [Cr(NH3)3Cl3], [Cr(NH3)6]Cl3, [Cr(NH3)5Cl]Cl2. OR (ii) Write two differences between primary and secondary valences in coordination compounds.
questions.correct_answer: (a) (i) [Cr(H2O)4Cl2]Cl. (ii) Secondary valency of Cr = 6 (coordination number). (b) Complex: does not dissociate into simple ions in solution. Double salt: dissociates completely into simple ions. (c)(i) Increasing conductivity: [Cr(NH3)3Cl3] (no ions) < [Cr(NH3)5Cl]Cl2 (2 ions) < [Cr(NH3)6]Cl3 (3 ions). OR (ii) Primary valency: ionisable, corresponds to oxidation state. Secondary valency: non-ionisable, corresponds to coordination number.
questions.solution_summary: Application of Werner's theory, distinction between complexes and double salts, and relationship between ionization and conductivity.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of Werner's theory, complex vs double salt, and conductivity trends.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) For a galvanic cell, the following half reactions are given. Decide, which will remain as reduction reaction and which will be reversed to become an oxidation reaction. Give reason for your answer. (I) Cr3+ + 3e- → Cr(s); E° = -0.74V (II) Fe2+ + 2e- → Fe(s); E° = -0.44V (ii) Represent the cell in which the following reaction takes place: Mg(s) + 2Ag+(0.001M) → Mg2+(0.100M) + 2Ag(s). Calculate Ecell if E°cell = 3.17V (log 10 = 1)
questions.correct_answer: (i) Fe2+/Fe has higher E° (-0.44V > -0.74V), so it will remain as reduction (cathode). Cr3+/Cr will be reversed to oxidation (anode). (ii) Cell: Mg(s) | Mg2+(0.100M) || Ag+(0.001M) | Ag(s). Ecell = E°cell - (0.059/n) log Q = 3.17 - (0.059/2) log ([Mg2+]/[Ag+]^2) = 3.17 - 0.0295 log (0.1/10^-6) = 3.17 - 0.0295 × 5 = 3.17 - 0.1475 = 3.0225 V.
questions.solution_summary: Identification of anode/cathode based on E° values, cell representation, and Nernst equation application.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of Nernst equation, cell representation, and comparison of electrode potentials.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) In a chemistry practical class, the teacher gave his students an amine 'X' having molecular formula C2H7N and asked the students to identify the type of amine. One of the students, Neeta, observed that it reacts with C6H5SO2Cl, to give a compound which dissolves in NaOH solution. Can you help Neeta to identify the compound 'X'? (ii) Arrange the following in the increasing order of their pKb value in aqueous phase: C6H5NH2, (CH3)2NH, NH3, CH3NH2, (CH3)3N. (iii) Aniline on nitration gives considerable amount of meta product along with ortho and para products. Why? (iv) Convert aniline to: (I) p-bromoaniline (II) phenol
questions.correct_answer: (i) X is ethylamine (CH3CH2NH2, primary amine). Hinsberg test: primary amine gives sulphonamide soluble in NaOH. (ii) Increasing pKb (decreasing basicity): (CH3)2NH < CH3NH2 < (CH3)3N < NH3 < C6H5NH2 (considering aqueous solvation effects). (iii) Aniline is highly activated; nitration gives a mixture of o-, m-, and p-nitroanilines, with m- product due to protonation of -NH2 under acidic conditions. (iv) (I) Aniline + Br2 in acetic acid → p-bromoaniline. (II) Aniline + NaNO2/HCl (0-5°C) → diazonium salt → H2O/heat → phenol.
questions.solution_summary: Identification of amine using Hinsberg test, ordering of pKb values, explanation of aniline nitration, and conversions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of Hinsberg test, pKb ordering with solvation effects, mechanism of aniline nitration, and multi-step conversions.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) When pyrolusite ore is fused with KOH, in presence of air, a dark green coloured product 'A' is obtained which changes to purple coloured compound 'B' in acidic medium. (I) Write the formulae of 'A' and 'B'. (II) Write the ionic equation for the reaction when compound 'B' reacts with Fe2+ in acidic medium. (ii) Give reasons: (I) Ce4+ in aqueous solution is a good oxidising agent. (II) The actinoid contraction is greater from element to element than lanthanoid contraction. (III) E°Zn2+/Zn value is more negative than expected, whereas E°Cu2+/Cu is positive.
questions.topic: Inorganic Chemistry - d- and f-Block Elements
questions.correct_answer: (i) A = K2MnO4 (potassium manganate, dark green), B = KMnO4 (potassium permanganate, purple). (II) MnO4^- + 8H+ + 5Fe2+ → Mn2+ + 5Fe3+ + 4H2O. (ii)(I) Ce4+ is a good oxidising agent because it has a strong tendency to gain an electron to attain stable Ce3+ (f1). (II) 5f electrons provide poorer shielding than 4f electrons, so actinoid contraction is greater. (III) Zn has a completely filled d10 configuration; its hydration enthalpy is low, making E° more negative. Cu has high hydration enthalpy and high ionisation enthalpy, making E° positive.
questions.solution_summary: Identification of manganate/permanganate, redox equation, and reasons for periodic trends and electrode potentials.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of preparation of KMnO4, redox chemistry of permanganate, and trends in d- and f-block elements.
